enrichment.short_description: Cacher is a cloud-based code snippet manager built for professional developers and engineering teams who need a fast, searchable home for reusable code. Launched in 2017 by a small SaaS team based in Boston, Massachusetts, Cacher lets individuals and teams create, organize, and share snippets across almost any programming language, then surface them instantly inside the editors and terminals they already use through native plugins for Visual Studio Code and IntelliJ-based IDEs, a browser extension, a Raycast integration, and a command-line interface. Snippets can be grouped into private or public libraries, tagged with labels, attached to files, and synced with GitHub Gists for teams that already lean on Git-based workflows. In August 2025 Cacher was acquired by Starr Holding Company, which has committed to keeping the product running under its existing brand while investing in further development, so the tool remains actively maintained rather than winding down. For teams tired of pasting the same boilerplate into Slack threads or losing snippets in scattered gists, Cacher centralizes that knowledge in one searchable, permissioned library.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cacher is a code snippet manager for developers and teams. See 2026 pricing, IDE integrations, pros and cons, and its acquisition by Starr Holding Company.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cacher is a code snippet management platform aimed at professional developers who accumulate reusable pieces of code, configuration, and scripts across projects and want a single organized, searchable place to keep them. The product launched on September 5, 2017 out of Boston, Massachusetts, and grew as a small, independently run SaaS business before being acquired by Starr Holding Company in August 2025. Starr has stated Cacher will continue operating under its existing brand while receiving new investment in engineering and marketing, which means the tool is actively maintained in 2026 rather than being sunset, an important distinction in a category where several competing snippet managers have shut down or stalled over the years. At its core, Cacher organizes code into snippets that can contain multiple files, syntax highlighting for dozens of languages, and descriptive labels for filtering. Snippets can be kept fully private, shared publicly on the snippets.cacher.io gallery, or shared within a team library with role-based access. What differentiates Cacher from a plain notes app or a folder of gists is its integration surface: dedicated plugins for Visual Studio Code and IntelliJ-based IDEs let developers insert snippets without leaving the editor, a browser extension brings the library to any web page, a Raycast integration serves power users on macOS, and a command-line interface lets snippets be scripted into build tools or dotfiles. Teams that already maintain code on GitHub can import and sync Gists directly into Cacher rather than re-entering everything by hand. Pricing follows a straightforward three-tier freemium model. The Free plan supports 15 private snippets and 3 private labels with unlimited public snippets, which is enough for a solo developer experimenting with the tool or maintaining a small public library. The Expert plan, at $9.99 per month when billed annually, removes those private caps entirely, adds the IDE plugins, and enables an ad-free Quick Create workflow, making it the natural upgrade for any individual developer who relies on Cacher daily. The Teams plan starts at $29.99 per month for the first five seats (billed annually, with additional seats at $8 per month) and adds shared team libraries, lightweight code review threads, activity notifications, and role management, with SSO available for larger teams or as a paid add-on for smaller ones. Organizations with strict data-residency or compliance requirements can license a self-hosted deployment for $5,000 per year plus $150 per seat per year. Overall, Cacher fits a specific niche: teams and individuals who want a dedicated, IDE-integrated snippet library rather than repurposing a general note-taking app, wiki, or their own private GitHub Gists.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Cacher Does and Who It Is For
paragraphs: Cacher solves a small but persistent developer annoyance: reusable code, configuration blocks, regex patterns, and shell one-liners tend to end up scattered across Slack messages, personal notes apps, and forgotten GitHub Gists. Cacher gives that content a permanent, searchable home with syntax highlighting for the vast majority of common programming and markup languages, plus support for multi-file snippets so a single entry can hold, say, a component file and its accompanying stylesheet together.

The product is aimed squarely at working developers and the teams they belong to, rather than at hobbyists or non-technical users. A freelance developer might use the Free plan to keep a personal library of go-to utility functions, while an engineering team on the Teams plan can use Cacher as a lightweight internal knowledge base for boilerplate, onboarding scripts, and internal API examples that would otherwise live nowhere in particular.

long_form_blocks.heading: Features and Editor Integrations
paragraphs: Cacher's strongest differentiator is how deeply it embeds into existing developer tooling rather than asking developers to open a separate web app every time they need a snippet. The Visual Studio Code and IntelliJ Platform plugins let users search and insert snippets without switching windows, the browser extension surfaces the library while working in any web-based tool, and the Raycast integration gives macOS users instant keyboard-driven access. A command-line interface rounds this out for developers who prefer to script snippet retrieval into their own workflows or dotfiles.

On the organizational side, snippets can be tagged with labels for filtering, kept private, shared publicly through Cacher's public snippet gallery, or shared within a team library with defined roles. Teams-tier customers get lightweight code review threads and activity notifications on top of the base snippet library, plus the option of single sign-on for larger seat counts. File attachment limits scale with plan, from 10 MB per file on the Free tier up to 100 MB per file on Teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Recent Ownership Change
paragraphs: Cacher uses a conventional freemium SaaS structure: a capped Free plan, an individual Expert plan at $9.99 per month billed annually, and a per-seat Teams plan starting at $29.99 per month for five seats with additional seats at $8 per month. A self-hosted option exists for larger organizations that need to keep snippet data on their own infrastructure, priced at $5,000 per year plus $150 per seat annually.

The most significant recent development is Cacher's August 2025 acquisition by Starr Holding Company, a SaaS holding group that has publicly stated its intent to keep the product operating under its current brand while investing further in development, engineering, and customer acquisition. For prospective buyers evaluating whether Cacher is a safe long-term bet, this acquisition is a reasonable signal of continuity rather than an imminent shutdown, though as with any recently acquired SaaS tool it is worth checking for product updates before committing to an annual plan.

faqs.question: Is Cacher still active in 2026?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cacher was acquired by Starr Holding Company in August 2025, and the acquirer has stated it intends to keep operating Cacher under its existing brand while investing further in the product, so it remains an actively maintained service.

faqs.question: How much does Cacher cost?
faqs.answer: Cacher offers a free plan with limited private snippets, an Expert plan at $9.99 per month (billed annually) for individuals, and a Teams plan starting at $29.99 per month for five seats plus $8 per month per additional seat. A self-hosted option costs $5,000 per year plus $150 per seat per year.

faqs.question: Does Cacher have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Free plan includes 15 private snippets, 3 private labels, unlimited public snippets, and access to the browser extension, Raycast integration, CLI, and GitHub Gist sync.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Cacher?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cacher offers a self-hosted deployment aimed at enterprises with data residency or compliance requirements, priced separately from the standard cloud plans.

faqs.question: Does Cacher integrate with Visual Studio Code?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cacher has an official VS Code extension (available on the Visual Studio Marketplace) that lets users browse and insert snippets directly inside the editor.

faqs.question: Can I import my GitHub Gists into Cacher?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cacher supports syncing and importing GitHub Gists so teams already using Gists can migrate their snippets without manually re-creating them.

faqs.question: Is Cacher a good fit for engineering teams, not just individuals?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Teams plan is specifically built for shared libraries, with role-based permissions, code review threads, and notifications, making it suitable for standardizing reusable code across a team.

faqs.question: What happened when Starr Holding Company acquired Cacher?
faqs.answer: In August 2025, Starr Holding Company acquired Cacher.io and announced the platform would continue operating under its existing brand with new investment in engineering and marketing rather than being shut down or merged into another product.

enrichment.short_description: Cacoo is a browser-based diagramming and flowchart tool made by Nulab Inc., the Fukuoka, Japan-based software company also known for the Backlog project management platform and the Typetalk team chat app. Released in 2010, Cacoo lets individuals and teams draw flowcharts, wireframes, network diagrams, mind maps, org charts, entity-relationship diagrams, and UML models in real time, with multiple people able to edit the same canvas simultaneously and watch each other's cursors move live. Because it runs entirely in the browser, Cacoo requires no desktop installation and works across Mac, Windows, and Linux, with editing also embeddable inside Google Workspace, Confluence, and other tools via official integrations. A free plan covers up to six diagram sheets for unlimited users, making Cacoo an easy way for small teams to try real-time collaborative diagramming before committing to a paid Pro or Team subscription. Because Cacoo comes from the same company behind Backlog, teams already using Nulab's project management suite can adopt it with a shared login and consistent design language, making it a natural fit for product, engineering, and design teams already inside the Nulab ecosystem.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription, priced per user per month with an unlimited-user free tier

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cacoo is an online diagramming and visual collaboration tool built by Nulab Inc., a Japanese software company founded in 2004 and headquartered in Fukuoka, with additional offices including Tokyo, Kyoto, New York, Amsterdam, and Singapore. Nulab is best known in Western markets for Backlog, its project management and issue-tracking tool, and Cacoo is the company's entry into visual collaboration, first released in 2010. The core idea behind Cacoo is real-time, multiplayer diagramming in the browser: several people can open the same flowchart, wireframe, network diagram, mind map, organizational chart, entity-relationship diagram, or UML model at once, see each other's cursors and edits live, and communicate through built-in video chat and comment threads without leaving the canvas. Because everything runs in a web browser rather than a native desktop application, Cacoo works identically on Mac, Windows, and Linux, and diagrams can be embedded into Confluence, Google Workspace documents, and other third-party tools through official integrations. Cacoo ships with a sizable library of templates and shape stencils spanning software architecture, network topology, floor plans, and standard flowchart symbols, along with a code-to-diagram feature that can generate certain diagram types from structured input, revision history so teams can roll back changes, and a lightweight built-in Kanban tool for basic task tracking alongside diagrams. Pricing follows a tiered subscription model typical of SaaS diagramming tools. The Free plan is genuinely free indefinitely, supports unlimited users, and includes six diagram sheets, templates and shapes, PNG export, real-time editing, in-app video chat, and comment threads, which is enough for a small team or an individual evaluating the tool. The Pro plan, at $6 per user per month (with roughly 17 percent savings on annual billing), removes the sheet cap, adds unlimited sheets, multiple export formats, revision history, and premium templates. The Team plan, also listed at $6 per user per month, builds on Pro with support for larger organizations of up to 1,000 users, one-on-one onboarding training, and password-protected diagrams. An Enterprise plan is available on custom pricing for organizations that need SAML single sign-on, SCIM user provisioning, audit logs, and centralized access management. A 14-day free trial is available on any paid plan without requiring a credit card. For teams already using Nulab's Backlog for project tracking, Cacoo's shared login and consistent design language make it a low-friction way to add diagramming into the same workflow, which is one of its more distinctive selling points compared with diagramming tools built by unrelated vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Cacoo Is and How It Fits Into Nulab's Product Line
paragraphs: Cacoo is the diagramming product inside Nulab Inc.'s suite of collaboration tools, sitting alongside Backlog (project management and issue tracking) and Typetalk (team chat). Nulab was founded in 2004 and is headquartered in Fukuoka, Japan, with a global user base built up over more than a decade of steady product development rather than venture-fueled rapid expansion. Cacoo itself launched in 2010 as a browser-based alternative to installed diagramming software, aimed at making flowcharts and wireframes something a whole team could co-edit rather than something one person built and emailed around.

Because Cacoo and Backlog share a parent company, organizations already using Backlog for sprints and issue tracking can bring diagramming into the same account structure, which reduces the usual friction of adopting a diagramming tool from an unrelated vendor. This is a meaningful differentiator versus standalone diagramming tools: Cacoo is designed to be one piece of a broader Nulab workflow rather than a single-purpose app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Real-Time Collaboration and Supported Diagram Types
paragraphs: Cacoo's headline feature is real-time multiplayer editing: multiple users can be on the same canvas simultaneously, see live cursor movement, and communicate through built-in video chat and threaded comments without switching to a separate call tool. Supported diagram types span flowcharts, wireframes and mockups, network diagrams, mind maps, organizational charts, entity-relationship diagrams, and UML models, backed by a library of templates and shape stencils.

Additional workflow features include revision history for rolling back unwanted changes, a code-to-diagram capability for generating certain diagrams from structured text, and a lightweight Kanban board for tracking related tasks alongside a diagram. Export options include PNG on the free tier, with additional formats unlocked on paid plans, and diagrams can be embedded directly into Confluence pages, Google Workspace documents, and other integrated tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Plan Structure
paragraphs: Cacoo's Free plan supports unlimited users and six diagram sheets, along with templates, PNG export, real-time editing, video chat, and comments, making it usable for genuinely small teams rather than a stripped-down trial. The Pro and Team plans are both listed at $6 per user per month, with the Team plan adding support for organizations up to 1,000 users, dedicated onboarding training, and password-protected diagrams on top of everything in Pro, such as unlimited sheets and revision history.

Annual billing saves roughly 17 percent compared with paying monthly, and a 14-day free trial is available on any paid plan without a credit card. Larger organizations that need SAML single sign-on, SCIM provisioning, audit logging, and centralized access management can move to the custom-priced Enterprise plan, which also includes a dedicated customer success manager.

enrichment.short_description: Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ: CDNS) is one of the world's largest electronic design automation (EDA) companies, providing the software, hardware, and IP that semiconductor and systems companies use to design integrated circuits, systems-on-chip, printed circuit boards, and increasingly full electronic systems. Headquartered in San Jose, California, Cadence traces its roots to the 1988 merger of SDA Systems and ECAD, and has since grown into a company with roughly 13,800 employees and multi-billion-dollar annual revenue. Its product portfolio spans digital and custom/analog IC design, verification (including hardware-assisted emulation and prototyping systems), PCB and multi-die packaging design, and, following its expansion into computational software, molecular modeling and simulation tools used by pharmaceutical and life sciences companies. Cadence competes primarily with Synopsys and Siemens EDA (formerly Mentor Graphics) in what is effectively an oligopoly at the center of the global chip industry, meaning nearly every major semiconductor company relies on Cadence, a competitor, or both to bring new chips to market. Because chip design tools are highly specialized, mission-critical, and sold through direct enterprise sales rather than self-serve signup, Cadence does not publish public pricing and instead licenses its software through custom multi-year agreements.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ: CDNS) makes electronic design automation software for chip and semiconductor design. See what it does, its history, and how licensing works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cadence Design Systems is a publicly traded American technology company, listed on NASDAQ under the ticker CDNS, that builds electronic design automation (EDA) software used to design, verify, and manufacture semiconductors and the electronic systems built around them. The company was formed in 1988 through the merger of two earlier EDA companies, SDA Systems and ECAD, and is headquartered in San Jose, California, in the heart of Silicon Valley. Over nearly four decades, Cadence has grown from a niche chip-design tools vendor into one of the two or three companies that essentially every major semiconductor and electronics company depends on, alongside its closest competitor Synopsys and, to a lesser degree, Siemens EDA (the former Mentor Graphics). As of the most recent public filings, Cadence employs roughly 13,800 people worldwide and reports annual revenue in the range of five billion dollars, with a market capitalization that has placed it among the larger software companies on public markets. Cadence's product portfolio is organized around the full lifecycle of chip design. On the digital design and implementation side, its tools handle logic synthesis, place-and-route, and physical design for advanced process nodes. On the custom and analog/mixed-signal side, tools like its Virtuoso platform are used for transistor-level circuit design, which is essential for anything from power management chips to high-speed interfaces. Verification is a major business line in its own right, including functional verification software as well as hardware-assisted emulation and prototyping systems that let engineers test chip designs before committing to expensive silicon fabrication. Cadence also provides printed circuit board (PCB) design and multi-die packaging tools, reflecting the industry's shift toward chiplet-based system design rather than single monolithic dies. In a notable diversification, Cadence has expanded into computational software for life sciences, offering molecular modeling, simulation, and drug discovery tools that apply the company's high-performance computing and simulation expertise outside of semiconductors. Because EDA software is mission-critical, highly specialized infrastructure that directly determines whether a multi-hundred-million-dollar chip design will function correctly, Cadence does not sell through a self-serve website or publish list pricing. Customers, typically semiconductor companies, systems companies, universities, and research labs, license Cadence's tools through direct sales relationships and multi-year enterprise agreements, often bundled with professional services, training, and support. For a directory audience researching Cadence, the most useful framing is that it is not a typical SaaS product with a pricing page, but rather foundational infrastructure software for the global chip industry, comparable in importance to Synopsys, and best evaluated by its product breadth, technology partnerships (including with major foundries like TSMC and Samsung), and its role in enabling the semiconductors that power essentially all modern computing and AI hardware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company Overview and History
paragraphs: Cadence Design Systems was formed in 1988 through the merger of SDA Systems and ECAD, two early electronic design automation companies, and has been headquartered in San Jose, California ever since. Over the following decades, Cadence grew primarily through a combination of internal product development and acquisitions of specialized EDA and computational software companies, positioning it as one of the anchor vendors in the semiconductor design tools industry.

Today Cadence trades on NASDAQ under the ticker CDNS, employs on the order of 13,800 people globally, and reports multi-billion-dollar annual revenue, making it one of the largest and most heavily documented companies in the enterprise software space. Its scale and public reporting requirements mean far more verifiable financial and operational detail is available about Cadence than about most tools in a typical SaaS directory.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core Product Areas
paragraphs: Cadence's software spans the full chip design and verification lifecycle: digital design and implementation tools for logic synthesis and physical design, custom and analog/mixed-signal design tools (such as its Virtuoso platform) for transistor-level circuit work, and functional verification software paired with hardware-assisted emulation and prototyping systems that let engineering teams validate designs before fabrication. The company also provides printed circuit board and multi-die packaging design tools, which have become increasingly important as chip designs move toward multi-chiplet architectures.

Beyond semiconductors, Cadence has diversified into computational software for the life sciences industry, applying its simulation and high-performance computing expertise to molecular modeling and drug discovery workflows for pharmaceutical companies. This diversification reflects a broader strategy of applying Cadence's core simulation and modeling technology to markets beyond its traditional chip-design customer base.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Cadence Is Licensed and Its Market Position
paragraphs: Unlike consumer or typical SMB SaaS products, Cadence does not publish self-serve pricing, and there is no public sign-up flow for its core EDA tools. Customers, overwhelmingly semiconductor and systems companies along with universities and research institutions, license Cadence software through direct enterprise sales relationships, usually structured as multi-year agreements that bundle software licenses with professional services, training, and support.

Cadence operates in what is effectively an oligopoly alongside Synopsys and Siemens EDA, meaning nearly every chip designed anywhere in the world touches tools from one of these three companies. This concentrated market structure, combined with the mission-critical nature of chip design software, gives Cadence significant customer stickiness and pricing power, but also means it is best understood as foundational industry infrastructure rather than a discretionary software purchase.

faqs.question: How much does Cadence software cost?
faqs.answer: Cadence does not publish public pricing. Its EDA tools are licensed through direct enterprise sales agreements, typically multi-year contracts negotiated with individual customers.

faqs.question: Who are Cadence's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Cadence's primary competitors are Synopsys and Siemens EDA (formerly Mentor Graphics), together forming the core of the global EDA industry.

faqs.question: Who actually uses Cadence's software?
faqs.answer: Cadence's customers are primarily semiconductor companies, systems and electronics manufacturers, and universities and research labs involved in chip design and verification.

faqs.question: Does Cadence only make chip design software?
faqs.answer: No. In addition to its core EDA products, Cadence also provides computational software for molecular modeling and simulation used by pharmaceutical and life sciences companies.

use_cases.description: A pharmaceutical research team applies Cadence's molecular modeling and simulation software to accelerate early-stage drug discovery workflows.

enrichment.short_description: Cairo-Dock is a free, open-source application launcher and dock for Linux desktops, designed to give users a customizable, animated bar of application icons similar in spirit to the docks found on macOS, but fully skinnable and desktop-environment-agnostic. Originally created by developer Fabrice Rey (known online as Fabounet) and first released in the mid-2000s (commonly dated to around 2007), Cairo-Dock is written in C and rendered using the Cairo graphics library combined with OpenGL, which lets it apply hardware-accelerated animations, reflections, and zoom effects while staying light on CPU usage. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 3, meaning the source code is freely available and the project is maintained by a small community of volunteer contributors on GitHub under the Cairo-Dock organization rather than by a commercial company. The dock works alongside virtually any Linux desktop environment (GNOME, KDE, Xfce, and others), supports a large catalog of downloadable themes and applets covering things like a clock, weather, system tray, Wi-Fi status, and email notifications, and can be detached into individual desktop widgets. Version 3.6 brought full Wayland support and HiDPI-aware rendering, keeping the project relevant as Linux distributions have shifted away from the older X11 display server.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open-source software; no paid tiers
enrichment.seo_title: Cairo-Dock: Free Customizable Linux Dock and App Launcher
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cairo-Dock is a free, open-source, GPU-accelerated application dock for Linux. See its features, themes, Wayland support, and how it compares to other launchers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cairo-Dock is a free and open-source application launcher and dock for Linux desktop environments, built to give users a macOS-style animated icon bar that is heavily customizable rather than tied to any single desktop environment's visual style. The project was created by developer Fabrice Rey, who goes by Fabounet in the open-source community, and dates back to the mid-2000s, with the project commonly cited as originating around 2007 before iterating through many major and minor releases over nearly two decades. It is written primarily in C and uses the Cairo 2D graphics library together with OpenGL for rendering, which allows it to apply hardware-accelerated visual effects such as zoom-on-hover, reflections, and smooth animations while keeping CPU usage low, an important design goal for a piece of software that runs continuously in the background of a desktop session. Cairo-Dock is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3, and its source code is maintained publicly on GitHub under the Cairo-Dock organization, with the broader community and documentation hosted at glx-dock.org. Because it has no corporate owner, there is no roadmap driven by commercial incentives; development pace has varied over the years depending on volunteer contributor availability, which is a normal characteristic of long-running open-source desktop projects. Functionally, Cairo-Dock acts as both a taskbar and an application launcher, arranging icons for pinned and running applications into one or more docks that can be positioned anywhere on screen and configured with different animation styles, icon sizes, and hide behaviors. What sets it apart from simpler launchers is its extensibility: a large catalog of applets can be added to the dock or detached to run as standalone desktop widgets, covering things like a clock, weather forecasts, a system tray, Wi-Fi and network status, an email notifier, and various visual effects add-ons. Dozens of ready-made themes are downloadable directly from within the application, letting users change the dock's entire look in a couple of clicks, and power users can go further with granular manual configuration of colors, transparency, icon behavior, and layout. A major milestone came with version 3.6, which added full support for the Wayland display protocol alongside the traditional X11 support Cairo-Dock has had since its inception, plus HiDPI-aware rendering so icons, fonts, and dock elements stay sharp on high-resolution 4K and higher displays. This Wayland work matters because most major Linux distributions have been defaulting to Wayland sessions in recent years, and desktop utilities that fail to add Wayland support risk becoming unusable for a growing share of users; Cairo-Dock's continued compatibility work is one of the clearer signs the project remains actively maintained rather than abandoned. Being free, open-source, and desktop-environment-agnostic, Cairo-Dock is typically installed by Linux users who want a more visually customizable alternative to their distribution's default taskbar, particularly on lightweight desktop environments like Xfce or on window-manager-only setups that lack a polished built-in launcher.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Cairo-Dock Is and Where It Came From
paragraphs: Cairo-Dock is an application launcher and taskbar dock for Linux, created by developer Fabrice Rey (Fabounet) and dating back to the mid-2000s, with the project commonly cited as originating around 2007. It takes its name from the Cairo 2D graphics library it uses for rendering, combined with OpenGL for hardware acceleration, which lets it produce fluid animations and effects while remaining relatively light on system resources compared with some competing dock implementations.

The project is free and open-source under the GNU General Public License version 3, with development coordinated on GitHub under the Cairo-Dock organization and community support hosted at glx-dock.org. As a volunteer-maintained project rather than a commercial product, its release cadence has ebbed and flowed over nearly two decades, but it has continued to receive updates, including significant modernization work for newer Linux display technology.

long_form_blocks.heading: Customization, Themes, and Applets
paragraphs: Cairo-Dock's defining characteristic is deep visual customization. Users can choose from a large library of downloadable themes to change the dock's entire appearance in a few clicks, or manually tune icon size, transparency, animation style, position, and auto-hide behavior. Multiple independent docks can be created at once, each with its own configuration, which is useful for separating pinned launchers from running-application indicators or system status widgets.

Beyond launching applications, Cairo-Dock supports a wide range of applets that add functionality like a clock, weather forecasts, a system tray, Wi-Fi and network monitoring, and email notifications. These applets can either live inside the dock itself or be detached to run as freestanding desktop widgets, giving Cairo-Dock a role that overlaps with both a taskbar and a lightweight widget engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Wayland Support and Continued Relevance
paragraphs: A significant recent milestone for Cairo-Dock was version 3.6, which added full support for the Wayland display protocol in addition to its long-standing X11 support. This matters because most major Linux distributions have shifted their default sessions to Wayland in recent years, and desktop utilities that never adapted to Wayland have effectively become unusable on modern default installs.

Version 3.6 also added HiDPI-aware rendering, ensuring icons, fonts, and dock elements stay crisp on 4K and higher-resolution displays rather than appearing blurry or oddly scaled. Together, this modernization work is a meaningful signal that Cairo-Dock remains an actively maintained project rather than a legacy tool left to bit-rot, even though it lacks a commercial backer.

faqs.question: What is Cairo-Dock?
faqs.answer: Cairo-Dock is a free, open-source application launcher and dock for Linux desktops, offering a customizable, animated icon bar that works across different desktop environments.

faqs.question: Is Cairo-Dock free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cairo-Dock is completely free and open-source, released under the GNU General Public License version 3, with no paid plans or premium tiers.

faqs.question: Who created Cairo-Dock?
faqs.answer: Cairo-Dock was created by developer Fabrice Rey, known online as Fabounet, and has since been maintained by a community of contributors.

faqs.question: Does Cairo-Dock work with Wayland?
faqs.answer: Yes, as of version 3.6, Cairo-Dock has full support for the Wayland display protocol in addition to its original X11 support.

faqs.question: Which Linux desktop environments does Cairo-Dock support?
faqs.answer: Cairo-Dock is desktop-environment agnostic and can run alongside GNOME, KDE, Xfce, and most other common Linux desktop environments.

faqs.question: Can I customize how Cairo-Dock looks?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cairo-Dock supports one-click downloadable themes as well as granular manual configuration of icon size, animation style, transparency, and position.

faqs.question: Is Cairo-Dock still actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project has continued to receive updates, including recent Wayland support and HiDPI rendering improvements, indicating ongoing community maintenance.

faqs.question: Is Cairo-Dock resource-intensive?
faqs.answer: No, it uses OpenGL hardware acceleration specifically to keep animations smooth while maintaining relatively low CPU usage compared with some alternative dock implementations.

enrichment.short_description: Caja is the default file manager for the MATE desktop environment, a Linux desktop environment that continues the look, feel, and codebase of GNOME 2 after GNOME itself moved to GNOME 3. Caja is a fork of GNOME's Nautilus file manager, forked at the same time MATE itself was forked from GNOME 2 around 2011, and it has been maintained since as a free, open-source component of the MATE project rather than as a standalone commercial product. It handles the everyday tasks users expect from a graphical file manager: browsing local folders, managing files and directories, accessing removable media, connecting to remote filesystems over protocols like SSH, FTP, and WebDAV, and rendering the desktop background and icons on distributions that use MATE as their desktop environment. Note that the name Caja is sometimes confused with the unrelated, now-discontinued Google Caja project, a JavaScript and HTML sandboxing framework for safely embedding third-party web content; Google archived that project in January 2021 and it has no connection to the MATE file manager described here. Caja is preinstalled on MATE-based distributions and spins such as Ubuntu MATE and Linux Mint MATE, and is developed publicly on GitHub under the mate-desktop organization.

enrichment.seo_title: Caja File Manager: MATE Desktop's Nautilus-Based File Browser Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Caja is the default file manager for the MATE Linux desktop, forked from GNOME's Nautilus. Learn its features and how it differs from the unrelated, discontinued Google Caja project.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Caja is the default graphical file manager bundled with the MATE desktop environment, a Linux desktop that was forked from GNOME 2 in 2011 after GNOME's own development moved to the very different GNOME 3 interface. When MATE forked GNOME 2's components to keep a traditional desktop metaphor alive, Caja was created at the same time as a fork of GNOME's Nautilus file manager as it existed at that point, and the two projects have since diverged, with Caja retaining and extending features from older Nautilus (such as a split-pane view, tree view, and emblem support) that were later removed from Nautilus itself as GNOME simplified its interface. Caja is free and open-source software, developed publicly on GitHub under the mate-desktop organization, and ships as the standard file manager on MATE-based Linux distributions and desktop spins, most notably Ubuntu MATE and Linux Mint's MATE edition. Functionally, Caja covers the core responsibilities users expect of a desktop file manager: browsing and organizing local files and folders, copying, moving, renaming, and deleting items, managing removable media such as USB drives and optical discs, and connecting to remote filesystems over protocols including SSH (SFTP), FTP, and WebDAV so that remote servers can be browsed like local folders. Beyond file browsing, Caja also plays a role as a core piece of the MATE desktop's shell: it is responsible for rendering the desktop background, placing and arranging desktop icons, and providing the visual entry points users click to open the trash, launch removable media, or access shortcuts to files and applications. It is worth explicitly disambiguating Caja from an unrelated project that shares the same name: Google Caja was a JavaScript and HTML/CSS sandboxing framework built by Google to let websites safely embed third-party content such as widgets and gadgets inside an isolated virtual iframe, based on object-capability security principles. That project was led by Jasvir Nagra with JavaScript sandboxing design work by Google researcher Mark S. Miller, and Google formally archived the Caja security project on January 31, 2021, citing known vulnerabilities and an inability to keep pace with modern web security research, recommending the Closure toolkit as a replacement. Google Caja has no technical or organizational relationship to the MATE Caja file manager described here, and given that Google's project has been discontinued for several years, the actively maintained MATE file manager is almost certainly the software being referenced when the name Caja is used in a current software or Linux desktop context. As with most desktop environment components, Caja is not sold or licensed separately; it is installed automatically as part of a MATE-based Linux distribution and has no independent pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Caja Is and Its Origins in MATE
paragraphs: Caja is the default file manager for the MATE desktop environment, a project that forked GNOME 2's codebase around 2011 to preserve a traditional desktop interface after GNOME moved to the substantially redesigned GNOME 3. As part of that fork, Caja began life as a copy of GNOME's Nautilus file manager at the point MATE branched off, and it has since been maintained independently by the MATE community rather than tracking upstream Nautilus changes.

Because of this shared ancestry, Caja retains a number of features that were present in older versions of Nautilus but later removed as GNOME simplified that application's interface, including emblem support for marking files, a split-pane dual-view mode, and a traditional tree view of the filesystem. These retained features are one of the main reasons some Linux users specifically prefer MATE and Caja over more modern, minimalist file managers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core File Management and Desktop Functionality
paragraphs: As a file manager, Caja handles the everyday operations users expect: browsing folders, organizing and renaming files, managing removable media like USB drives and optical discs, and connecting to remote filesystems over SSH, FTP, and WebDAV so remote servers can be navigated the same way as local folders. It also integrates with the MATE trash system and standard desktop conventions like drag-and-drop file operations.

Beyond file browsing, Caja also functions as a core piece of the MATE desktop shell itself, responsible for rendering the desktop background and desktop icons on systems where MATE is the active desktop environment. This dual role, file manager and desktop shell component, mirrors the role Nautilus historically played in GNOME 2, which is consistent with Caja's origins as a direct fork of that codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Disambiguation: This Is Not Google Caja
paragraphs: Anyone researching Caja should be aware that the name is shared with an unrelated Google project, commonly called Google Caja, which was a JavaScript and HTML/CSS sandboxing framework designed to let websites safely embed third-party widgets in an isolated virtual iframe based on object-capability security principles. That project, associated with researchers Jasvir Nagra and Mark S. Miller, was formally archived by Google on January 31, 2021 due to known vulnerabilities and a lack of resources to keep it current with modern web security research.

The two projects share nothing beyond a name: Google Caja was a web security sandboxing library, discontinued for several years, while the MATE Caja file manager described in this profile is an actively maintained, open-source Linux desktop application. Given that Google's project has been dead since 2021, references to Caja in a current desktop-software or Linux context should be understood as referring to the MATE file manager rather than the defunct Google security project.

faqs.question: What is Caja?
faqs.answer: Caja is the default file manager for the MATE Linux desktop environment. It is a fork of GNOME's Nautilus file manager, created when MATE itself was forked from GNOME 2.

faqs.question: Is Caja the same as Google Caja?
faqs.answer: No. Google Caja was an unrelated JavaScript and HTML sandboxing security project that Google archived in January 2021. The MATE file manager Caja has no connection to it.

faqs.question: Is Caja free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Caja is free and open-source software, distributed as a core component of the MATE desktop environment at no cost.

faqs.question: Which Linux distributions include Caja?
faqs.answer: Caja is preinstalled on MATE-based distributions and desktop spins, most notably Ubuntu MATE and the MATE edition of Linux Mint.

faqs.question: Does Caja support connecting to remote servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, Caja can browse remote filesystems over protocols including SSH (SFTP), FTP, and WebDAV alongside local files.

faqs.question: How is Caja different from Nautilus?
faqs.answer: Caja began as a fork of Nautilus but has since diverged, retaining older Nautilus features like emblems, split-pane view, and tree view that were later removed from Nautilus as GNOME simplified its interface.

faqs.question: Is Google Caja still active?
faqs.answer: No, Google formally archived the Google Caja project on January 31, 2021, citing known vulnerabilities and recommending the Closure toolkit as a replacement.

faqs.question: Can I install Caja on a non-MATE desktop?
faqs.answer: Caja can technically be installed on other Linux desktops, but it is designed and primarily used as the default file manager within the MATE desktop environment.

enrichment.short_description: calcurse is a free, open-source, text-based calendar and scheduling application built for the terminal on Unix-like systems including Linux, macOS, BSD variants, and other Unix derivatives. It was created in 2004 by Frederic Culot, who began developing it while finishing his PhD in astrophysics as a personal tool for keeping track of appointments and to-do items, and its name is a portmanteau of calendar and ncurses, the terminal UI library it is built on. Culot released calcurse under the permissive BSD license, and since 2011 the project has been maintained by Lukas Fleischer, with many core features added since 2017 designed and implemented by contributor Lars Henriksen. The application splits its terminal interface into three panels, a monthly calendar view, a list of appointments for the selected day, and a to-do list, all navigable with configurable, largely vi-style keyboard shortcuts. It supports recurring and all-day events, attached notes opened in the user's preferred external text editor, a configurable notification system for upcoming deadlines, import and export in the standard iCalendar format, and experimental CalDAV support for syncing with mobile devices and other calendar services. Because it runs entirely inside a terminal with no graphical dependencies, calcurse is popular among developers and sysadmins who live in the command line and want scheduling that fits into scriptable, keyboard-driven workflows rather than switching to a separate graphical calendar app.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: calcurse is a free, open-source, keyboard-driven calendar and task manager for the terminal. Learn its history, features, and how it compares to graphical calendars.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: calcurse is a text-based calendar, scheduling, and to-do list application designed to run entirely inside a terminal on Unix-like operating systems, including Linux, macOS, and the BSD family (OpenBSD, FreeBSD, and NetBSD). It was created in 2004 by Frederic Culot, a developer who was finishing his PhD in astrophysics at the time and built the tool originally for his own use to manage an increasingly complicated schedule of appointments and tasks, before releasing it publicly under the permissive BSD license. The name calcurse combines calendar with ncurses, the terminal-based user interface library the application is built on, which is a fitting description of what the software actually is: a full calendar and task manager that never requires leaving the command line or opening a graphical window. Culot maintained the project in its early years, and since 2011 primary maintenance has been carried on by Lukas Fleischer, with numerous core features added from 2017 onward designed and implemented by contributor Lars Henriksen, reflecting the kind of small, dedicated volunteer maintainer team typical of long-lived Unix command-line utilities. The interface is organized into three panels visible at once: a monthly calendar grid in one corner, a list of the current day's scheduled appointments in another, and a to-do list at the bottom for tracking tasks that aren't tied to a specific time. Navigation uses configurable keyboard shortcuts that default to a scheme familiar to vi and vim users, letting experienced terminal users move through days, add events, and check off tasks without touching a mouse. Functionally, calcurse supports both one-off and recurring appointments, all-day events, and the ability to attach freeform notes to any calendar item, which open in the user's preferred external text editor rather than a built-in note field. A configurable notification system can alert users to upcoming deadlines and appointments, which is particularly useful given the software has no persistent graphical presence to glance at. For interoperability, calcurse can import and export calendar data in the standard iCalendar (.ics) format used by virtually every mainstream calendar application, and it includes experimental CalDAV support, letting it synchronize with mobile devices and CalDAV-compatible calendar servers rather than existing as a fully isolated island of data. calcurse also supports command-line-only, non-interactive operation, meaning its data can be filtered, formatted, and queried through command-line flags, making it scriptable for use in shell scripts, status bars, or automated reminder systems rather than only through its interactive curses interface. calcurse's target audience is narrow but well-defined: developers, system administrators, and other terminal-centric users who prefer to manage their schedule without leaving a terminal multiplexer session, and who value calcurse's minimal resource footprint, scriptability, and plain-text-adjacent data storage over the visual polish of a graphical or web-based calendar application.

long_form_blocks.heading: History and Origins
paragraphs: calcurse was created in 2004 by Frederic Culot, who built the tool for his own use while finishing his PhD in astrophysics and needing a better way to manage an increasingly complex schedule of appointments and tasks. He later released it publicly under the permissive BSD license, allowing free use, modification, and redistribution.

The project transitioned to new maintainership in 2011 under Lukas Fleischer, who has continued to guide its development, and many core features added since 2017 were designed and implemented by contributor Lars Henriksen. This lineage of dedicated maintainers over roughly two decades is characteristic of durable Unix command-line tools that outlive their original creators' direct involvement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Interface and Everyday Workflow
paragraphs: calcurse's terminal interface is split into three simultaneously visible panels: a monthly calendar, a list of the selected day's appointments, and a to-do list. This layout gives users a full overview of their schedule and outstanding tasks without needing to switch between separate screens, and navigation uses configurable, largely vi-style keyboard shortcuts that terminal-focused users tend to find fast and natural.

Notes can be attached to any calendar entry and are opened in the user's preferred external text editor rather than a built-in note field, keeping calcurse consistent with the broader Unix philosophy of composing small, focused tools rather than reimplementing a text editor internally. A configurable notification system can also alert users to approaching deadlines even when they aren't actively looking at the calcurse interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Interoperability, CalDAV, and Scripting
paragraphs: Because calcurse users often need to coordinate with people and services using mainstream calendar tools, the application supports importing and exporting data in the standard iCalendar (.ics) format, and includes experimental CalDAV support for synchronizing with mobile devices and CalDAV-compatible servers. This keeps calcurse from being a data silo even though its primary interface is text-based.

calcurse also runs in a non-interactive, command-line mode, with options for filtering and formatting appointment and to-do data. This makes it usable inside shell scripts, terminal status bars, or automated notification pipelines, extending its usefulness beyond the interactive curses interface into broader terminal-based automation workflows.

faqs.question: What is calcurse?
faqs.answer: calcurse is a free, open-source, text-based calendar and scheduling application that runs in the terminal on Unix-like systems such as Linux, macOS, and the BSDs.

faqs.question: Who created calcurse?
faqs.answer: calcurse was created in 2004 by Frederic Culot. Since 2011 it has been maintained by Lukas Fleischer, with significant contributions from Lars Henriksen since 2017.

faqs.question: Is calcurse free?
faqs.answer: Yes, calcurse is free and open-source software, released under the permissive BSD license.

faqs.question: Does calcurse sync with other calendars?
faqs.answer: calcurse supports importing and exporting the standard iCalendar (.ics) format and has experimental CalDAV support for syncing with mobile devices and compatible calendar servers.

faqs.question: Can I use calcurse without a graphical interface?
faqs.answer: Yes, calcurse is designed to run entirely inside a terminal and also supports a non-interactive command-line mode for scripting.

faqs.question: What does the name calcurse mean?
faqs.answer: The name combines calendar with ncurses, the terminal user interface library that calcurse is built on.

faqs.question: Does calcurse support recurring appointments?
faqs.answer: Yes, calcurse supports both recurring appointments and all-day events alongside standard one-off appointments.

faqs.question: Which operating systems support calcurse?
faqs.answer: calcurse runs on most Linux distributions, macOS, OpenBSD, FreeBSD, and NetBSD, with packages available in most major package managers.

enrichment.short_description: Calendar Icons Generator is a free, browser-based web tool that creates a full year's worth of custom, dated calendar-style icons in a single click, designed primarily for people who want personalized daily folder or app icons in tools like Notion, Windows folders, and mobile home screens. Built with Next.js by an independent developer, the tool grew out of the creator's own use of Notion for journaling and project tracking, where distinct, date-specific icons make it easier to visually navigate daily pages at a glance. Users design one icon template, choosing background, header, outline, and font colors, selecting a date format (such as 1, 01, or 1st) and a day-label style (full name, abbreviation, or single initial), and adjusting font style and size, with a live preview showing how the icon looks across a full month before committing. Selecting a target year and clicking generate produces a downloadable zip file containing 365 (or 366 in a leap year) individually dated icon images, ready to drop into a Notion icon library, a folder-naming workflow, or any other system that supports custom per-item icons. Because the tool is explicitly free with no account, subscription, or payment required, it functions more as a niche utility and portfolio project than a commercial SaaS product, and it should be evaluated on convenience and customization depth rather than pricing or support tiers.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Calendar Icons Generator is a small, free, browser-based utility that solves a specific and fairly niche design problem: generating a complete year's worth of individually dated calendar-style icons from a single design template. Rather than manually creating 365 separate icon images to represent each day of the year, a user designs one icon once, and the tool automatically produces the full set with the correct day-of-week label and date stamped onto each image. The tool is built using Next.js and was created by an individual developer rather than a company or design agency; according to the creator, the idea emerged from personal use of Notion for daily journaling and project management, where giving each daily page a distinct, date-labeled icon makes it noticeably easier to visually scan and navigate a long list of dated entries compared with using the same generic icon repeated hundreds of times. The design workflow is built around a live preview: users adjust the background color, header color, outline color, and font colors, choose how the date itself should be displayed (as a bare number like 1, a zero-padded number like 01, or an ordinal like 1st), and choose how the day-of-week label appears, whether as a full name such as Monday, a short abbreviation such as Mon, or a single initial such as M. Font style and size can be adjusted for both the header and date text, including the option to use any font already installed on the user's own system by typing in its name, and a dark mode is available in the design interface itself for users working in low-light conditions. Once a design is finalized, the user selects which calendar year they want icons generated for and clicks a single generate button, which produces a downloadable zip archive containing an individual icon image for every day of that year, correctly labeled with its date and day of the week. The tool explicitly markets this as a one-click, entirely free process, with no account creation, payment, or subscription required at any point, positioning it closer to a free utility or indie side project than a commercial SaaS product. The generated icons are intended primarily for three common use cases that the tool's own site calls out: customizing daily pages inside Notion (a popular use case given the tool's origin story), renaming and re-icon-ing folders on Windows to visually distinguish dated folders, and setting custom icons for date-specific items on iOS or Android mobile devices. Because Calendar Icons Generator is free, has no growth-stage funding, company structure, or public pricing history to research, and is maintained by a single developer rather than a team, it is best understood and evaluated in this directory as a lightweight, purpose-built utility rather than as an enterprise or venture-backed software product; its value proposition rests entirely on how much time it saves compared with manually creating or sourcing 365 individually dated icon graphics.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Calendar Icons Generator Does
paragraphs: Calendar Icons Generator automates a narrow but genuinely tedious design task: producing a full year of individually dated calendar-style icons from a single design template. Instead of manually creating or sourcing 365 separate icon images, a user designs one icon once inside the tool's browser-based editor and the application generates the complete dated set automatically for a chosen year.

The tool was built with Next.js by an independent developer, and according to the creator's own account, the idea originated from personal use of Notion for daily journaling and project management, where giving each dated page a visually distinct icon makes long lists of entries noticeably easier to scan than reusing one generic icon everywhere.

long_form_blocks.heading: Customization Options and Output Format
paragraphs: The design interface lets users adjust background, header, outline, and font colors, choose between date formats such as 1, 01, or 1st, and select how the day-of-week label is displayed, whether as a full name, an abbreviation, or a single initial. Font style and size can be customized for both header and date text, including support for any font already installed on the user's own system, and a live preview shows how the design will look across a full month before the full year is generated.

Once satisfied with the design, the user picks a target calendar year and generates the full set with one click, producing a downloadable zip file containing individually dated icon images for every day of that year. The site explicitly positions Notion daily pages, Windows folder customization, and mobile device home screens (iOS and Android) as the primary use cases for the resulting icon set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cost and How to Think About This Tool
paragraphs: Calendar Icons Generator is entirely free, with no account creation, subscription, or payment required at any point in the workflow, which sets it apart from most tools covered in a typical software directory. It should be evaluated less like a growth-stage SaaS product with pricing tiers and support plans, and more like a well-executed, purpose-built free utility or independent developer side project.

For anyone specifically searching for a way to generate a full year of custom dated icons for Notion, Windows folders, or mobile devices without paying for design software or manually creating hundreds of images, Calendar Icons Generator directly addresses that need with a straightforward, no-cost workflow.

enrichment.short_description: Calendarific is a developer-focused API that provides structured public holiday and calendar data covering more than 230 countries, including national holidays, regional and local observances, and religious holidays, updated on a regular schedule. Developers integrate Calendarific into applications such as booking platforms, HR and payroll systems, e-commerce checkout flows, and scheduling tools that need to know when a given country or region observes a holiday, rather than hand-maintaining static holiday lists that quickly go stale. The API returns holiday data as structured JSON, with support for filtering by country, year, and holiday type, and premium plans add features like configurable alerts, CSV/XLS export, and multiple API keys for separating environments. Calendarific offers a genuinely usable free tier capped at 500 API calls per month, with paid Starter, Business, and Enterprise plans scaling up to tens of millions of calls per month for higher-traffic applications; subscription billing is processed through Stripe. Because holiday rules change from year to year and vary enormously by country, religion, and local jurisdiction, an API like Calendarific saves development teams from the ongoing maintenance burden of tracking those changes themselves.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Calendarific is an API product built specifically to solve one recurring developer problem: reliably knowing when public holidays, religious observances, and regional commemorations fall in a given country or region, without having to hand-build and continually update that data internally. The API covers holiday data for more than 230 countries, spanning national public holidays, local and regional observances, and religious holidays, and returns results as structured JSON that can be filtered by country, year, and holiday type. This kind of data matters more than it might initially seem for a wide range of applications: booking and travel platforms need to know which days count as holidays when calculating availability and pricing, HR and payroll systems need accurate holiday calendars per country to calculate pay and time off correctly, e-commerce platforms may adjust shipping estimates or promotions around holidays, and scheduling or calendar apps need holiday data to correctly mark non-working days for users in different countries. Building and maintaining this data set internally is a genuinely difficult, ongoing task, since holiday rules vary by country, can shift from year to year (particularly for religious holidays tied to lunar calendars), and require regular verification against official government sources, which is the core value proposition Calendarific is selling: someone else does that maintenance work so client applications don't have to. Calendarific's pricing follows a freemium, usage-based subscription model with clearly published tiers. The Free plan allows 500 API calls per month at no cost, but requires attribution back to Calendarific and offers only community support, limited historical and upcoming data, and quarterly data updates, along with only single-language holiday names. The Starter plan removes the attribution requirement and adds email support, holiday names in national languages, complete historical and upcoming data, and quarterly updates, priced at $12 per month on monthly billing or $100 per year on annual billing (a modest discount for committing annually), with a call limit of 10,000 requests per month. The Business plan, available only on annual billing at $500 per year, raises the limit to 50,000 calls per month and adds priority support, all supported languages, weekly data updates, and a dedicated account manager. At the top end, an Enterprise plan priced at $4,000 per year (annual billing only) supports up to 50,000,000 calls per month with dedicated support and daily data updates, aimed at very high-traffic applications such as large travel or e-commerce platforms. All paid plans include CSV and XLS export options, premium API parameters, multiple API key support for separating development and production environments, and SLA coverage, and subscription payments are processed through Stripe. For a development team evaluating whether to build holiday-awareness in-house or use a service like Calendarific, the free tier is generous enough to validate the integration during development, while the Starter plan's $12-a-month entry point is inexpensive relative to the engineering time required to build and maintain equivalent holiday data internally, which is the core case for adopting the API rather than reinventing it.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Calendarific Does and Why Holiday Data Is Hard
paragraphs: Calendarific is an API that supplies structured public holiday and calendar data for more than 230 countries, covering national holidays, regional and local observances, and religious holidays, returned as JSON that developers can filter by country, year, and holiday type. The underlying problem it solves is less trivial than it sounds: holiday rules differ enormously between countries, change over time as governments add or adjust observances, and for many religious holidays are determined by lunar or lunisolar calendars that shift dates from year to year rather than falling on a fixed date.

Applications that need to know when holidays occur, booking and travel platforms, HR and payroll systems, e-commerce sites adjusting shipping or promotions, and calendar and scheduling tools, would otherwise need to build and continually maintain this data set internally, verifying it against official government sources on an ongoing basis. Calendarific centralizes that maintenance work into a single API so client applications can query up-to-date holiday data instead of hand-maintaining static lists that inevitably go stale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Tiers and Usage Limits
paragraphs: Calendarific's Free plan allows 500 API calls per month with attribution required, community support only, single-language holiday names, and quarterly data updates, which is enough to prototype an integration but not to run it in production for most applications. The Starter plan removes the attribution requirement, adds email support and complete historical and upcoming holiday data in national languages, and is priced at $12 per month billed monthly or $100 per year billed annually, with a 10,000 call per month limit.

Higher tiers are only available on annual billing: the Business plan costs $500 per year for 50,000 calls per month with priority support, all languages, weekly data updates, and a dedicated account manager, while the Enterprise plan costs $4,000 per year for up to 50,000,000 calls per month with dedicated support and daily updates. All paid tiers include CSV/XLS export, premium API parameters, multiple API keys, and SLA coverage, with billing processed through Stripe.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Use Calendarific
paragraphs: Calendarific is best suited to development teams building applications where holiday-awareness materially affects the product experience or business logic, such as international booking platforms calculating availability, payroll and HR systems computing time off across multiple countries, or e-commerce platforms adjusting delivery estimates around holidays. The free tier is a reasonable way to validate the integration during development before committing to a paid plan.

Because pricing scales cleanly from a $12-per-month Starter tier up to a $4,000-per-year Enterprise tier supporting tens of millions of monthly calls, Calendarific can fit both small side projects and high-traffic production applications, with the main decision point being how many holiday lookups the application needs per month and whether features like weekly or daily data update frequency and priority support justify the higher-tier pricing.
